skill.name: azure-mgmt-botservice-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Bot Service Management SDK for Python to create bots, manage channels, configure OAuth connections, and inspect bot resources.
skill.summary: Manage Azure Bot Service resources with Python SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many executable and dangerous-pattern signals, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, Python import examples, inline method names, placeholders, and Azure SDK documentation. The remaining confirmed concern is a documentation example that prints Direct Line channel keys, which could leak secrets if copied into logs or shared terminal output.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Example Prints Direct Line Secrets
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The List Channel Keys example retrieves Direct Line channel keys and prints each key to stdout. This is dangerous because copied example code can expose bearer-style channel secrets in terminals, CI logs, or chat transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly calls list_with_keys and prints site.key. The code is documentation rather than an auto-executed script, so impact depends on user execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command findings are caused by fenced bash and Python examples, plus inline backticked method names in tables. The skill file does not define an executable script or hidden command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected locations are Markdown examples and inline documentation. No evidence found of autonomous shell execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Python Import Blocks Read as Dynamic Imports
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The dynamic import findings are ordinary Python from-import blocks for Azure model classes. They do not evaluate user input or load modules dynamically at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a static Python import statement in a documentation block. There is no import expression with variable-controlled module names.

low_findings.title: Low Risk: Environment Variables Used for Azure Configuration
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The examples read AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID and AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP from the environment. This is normal Azure SDK configuration, but users should avoid exposing environment-derived values in shared output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable names are configuration identifiers, not passwords. The examples are legitimate, but environment access is still relevant for operational guidance.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder Endpoint and Weak Crypto Signals
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URL is a placeholder Azure App Service endpoint for a bot callback. Weak cryptography findings appear to match descriptive text and identifiers, with no evidence found of cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is illustrative and no secret is sent to it in the example. The weak-crypto alerts do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or signature code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Critical Combination Heuristic
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The critical heuristic combined documentation examples for installation, Azure SDK calls, environment variables, and placeholders. No evidence found of obfuscation, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or concealed execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found a single Markdown skill file with transparent Azure SDK examples. The file contains no hidden payloads or instructions to bypass security analysis.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Material Printed in Example Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates printing Direct Line channel keys. Marketplace users should replace this with masked output or secure secret handling before reuse.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines access site.key and pass it directly to print. The pattern is clearly present in the reviewed file.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Bot Service with Python
content.value_statement: Azure Bot Service setup often requires many SDK calls across bots, channels, and OAuth connections. This skill gives focused Python examples for creating, updating, listing, and configuring those resources.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-mgmt-botservice and azure-identity packages.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential setup with subscription and resource group variables.
Provides examples for creating, reading, updating, listing, and deleting bot resources.
Documents channel configuration for Microsoft Teams, Direct Line, and Web Chat.
Shows OAuth connection setting creation and listing for bot services.
Summarizes common SDK operation groups, SKU options, and supported channel classes.
limitations: It is a reference skill, not a runnable automation package.
Examples require an Azure subscription, resource group, permissions, and installed SDK packages.
Some snippets use placeholders that must be replaced with real application values.
The key-listing example should be adapted to avoid printing secrets.

use_cases.title: Provision a Development Bot
use_cases.description: Use the examples to create an F0 Azure Bot resource and verify its endpoint and metadata.

use_cases.title: Configure Bot Channels
use_cases.description: Reference the Teams, Direct Line, and Web Chat snippets when enabling channels for a bot service.

use_cases.title: Review SDK Operations
use_cases.description: Use the operation table and connection examples to plan management scripts for bot resources.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Bot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Azure Bot Service skill to outline the steps for creating a Python-managed development bot with the F0 SKU.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Channel
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the channel configuration guidance for adding Microsoft Teams and Web Chat to an existing Azure Bot resource.

prompt_templates.title: Plan OAuth Connections
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design the Azure Bot Service OAuth connection settings for Microsoft Graph access without exposing client secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Bot Management Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Bot Service management workflow for safe credential handling, channel scope, SKU choice, and destructive operations.

output_examples.input: User asks how to create a new Azure Bot Service resource with Python.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering package installation, DefaultAzureCredential authentication, required environment variables, bot properties, SKU selection, and create operation validation.

output_examples.input: User asks how to enable Direct Line and Web Chat channels.
output_examples.output: A channel configuration checklist with required channel classes, site settings, version choices, and a warning to avoid printing generated keys.

output_examples.input: User asks what SDK areas are available for bot management.
output_examples.output: A plain-language summary of bots, channels, bot connections, Direct Line, email, operations, and host settings capabilities.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and managed identity where possible.
Store app IDs, client secrets, and channel keys in Azure Key Vault or another secret manager.
Enable only required channels and review CORS, scopes, and key rotation before production.
anti_patterns: Do not print Direct Line keys, OAuth secrets, or subscription details in logs.
Do not copy placeholder endpoints or client IDs into production without validation.
Do not run delete operations from examples without confirming the target resource group and bot name.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps generate and adapt Python SDK examples for managing Azure Bot Service resources.

faq.question: Does it deploy a complete bot application?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Azure Bot Service management resources, channels, and connection settings.

faq.question: Which authentication method does it show?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential from azure-identity, using Azure environment and identity configuration.

faq.question: Can it configure bot channels?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for Microsoft Teams, Direct Line, and Web Chat channels.

faq.question: What security caution applies to this skill?
faq.answer: Do not print or share Direct Line keys, OAuth secrets, or other generated credentials.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
